data.title: MCI At 35% Premium - A Good Opportunity To Close Some Profit
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

In this short article, we will focus on Barings Corporate Investors (NYSE:MCI) and try to determine whether there are any substantial reasons behind its recent sharp price appreciation.

First offered to the public in 1971, Barings Corporate Investors (MCI) seeks to provide a consistent yield while at the same time offering an opportunity for capital gains. MCI's principal investments are in the privately placed, below-investment grade, long-term debt obligations of companies primarily domiciled in the U.S. These direct placement securities may in some cases be accompanied by equity features such as warrants, conversion rights and occasionally preferred stocks. We typically purchase these investments directly from their issuers, who tend to be smaller companies, and may also temporarily invest in marketable investment-grade and other debt securities (including high yield) and common stocks. MCI is managed on a total return basis, and distributes substantially all of its net income each year in the form of quarterly dividends.

Source: Company website

MCI Portfolio

Overview

In building the Fund's portfolio, MCI's managers incorporate a strategy of investing predominantly in privately placed long-term debt obligations with sub-investment grade credit quality and a smaller percent of equity exposition in "common stock, preferred stock, warrants, conversion rights, or other equity features". This is what the portfolio allocations look like as of the Third Quarter of 2024:

If we examine the Fund's portfolio composition more closely, we see that it includes more than 400 holdings, with the top ten making up more than 17% of the exposure. The Sector Allocation of the portfolio holdings shows diversification across the industrial sectors, with the largest share of securities coming from the Information Technology sector.

Capital structure and expenses

This is what the assets and liabilities of the fund look like as of the end of September 2024 - the last report that can be found on the sponsor's website. The effective leverage of the portfolio is calculated to be 9.9%

According to CEFConnect, the effective leverage as of 2/20/2025 is 11.48%:

It should come as no surprise that with such low leverage, the Fund's interest expenses are also low at 0.61%, with 1.65% total expenses annually:

The ratio of interest expense to net assets has increased in 2022 compared to previous years with the hike in the Fed Funds and stayed elevated since.

The treasury yield curve is finally normalizing after a long period of inverted yields. With the forward estimates of short-term rates further going lower, we may expect that the cost of financing to decrease and gradually return to its average levels from before 2022.

Premium/Discount

The charts above depict the growth of the price and NAV of MCI for 10 years, compared to the average of a peer group, according to the CEFData calculations. The upside deviation in the price that started at the beginning of 4Q 2024 has escalated in the past couple of days. As there are no official financial statements of the sponsor since the 3Q report, we have to rely on the last estimated value for the NAV.

The market close price of the MCI on 2/21/2025 was $23.17, so we can calculate a premium of 34.6%.

With an average discount of 2.5% for the past three years and 4.2% for the past five, we can assess that the fund is way overvalued at this moment at close to 35% premium, or 40% above its average pricing for the past 5 years.

However, if we look at the premium/discount history of MCI since its inception, we see that the current sharp price increase is not something new for the fund. On several occasions through the years, the price spiked and it took some time before mean reverting.

MCI Performance

Dividends

The Trust distributes substantially all of its net income to shareholders each year. Accordingly, the Trust pays dividends to shareholders four times per year. The Trust pays dividends to its shareholders in cash, unless the shareholder elects to participate in the Dividend Reinvestment and Share Purchase Plan.

Source: 3Q Report

The fund is paying regular quarterly and occasionally quarterly distributions, as the TTM dividend amounts to $1.69. With the current price of the stock at $23.17, that corresponds to a dividend yield of 7.29%. This is what the dividend yield looks like for the past ten years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and the dividend yield:

The annualized total returns of MCI over the last 10 years are around 9.3% on NAV and 11.1% on price:

The long-term distribution yield of the fund is pretty much in line with the portfolio total returns for the same time frame. This is a good indication for the shareholders, as it quite possibly means that capital depletion is not on the table.

This is what the total return of the fund looks like compared to SP500 over the last 10 years:

After the COVID crisis, MCI has consistently underperformed SP500 on its total return metric, although following the same general trend. It is only in the past couple of weeks that the fund outperformed the market, due to its sharp spike in price. Let us take another look at the Annualized Total Returns bar chart above. For the past three and six months, the total return on NAV has been close to zero, while the price continued its strong uptrend, reaching the point of outperforming SP500 in the last few days. For me, this is a clear overvaluation. To put the words in a picture, below is shown what the MCI total return for the last six months looks like, compared to SP500. I could not find a way to include the NAV return in the chart, but it is close to zero for this time frame.

We can additionally compare the long-term performance of MCI with that of its "brother" CEF MassMutual Participation Investors (MPV). Both funds have almost identical holdings through the many years of their existence and it should come as no surprise the hand-in-hand total returns they have.

We can "zoom" to the last twelve-month total return and the upside deviation of MCI becomes clearly visible:

If we take a look at the price performance of both funds we see an additional confirmation - at different times one of the CEFs outperforms the other, but they eventually always mean revert.

Conclusion

MCI is a solid closed-end fund with a management team that has built a fixed-income portfolio returning over 9% on NAV for the past ten years. The relatively low leverage and distributions in line with what the holdings return are qualities we'd like to see in more CEFs.

Apparently, a lot of investors think like this, as the price appreciation of MCI in the last months and especially in the last days shows. At the moment, nothing indicates it is supported by appreciation of the NAV of the fund, but we'll have to wait for the 4Q report for this to be completely sure.

A fun suggestion, discussed in our Trade With Beta chat room is that the buying pressure could be a sympathy trade due to the similarity in the ticker with Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI). Whatever the reason for the 35% premium at the moment, if the investors think there is no sound financial logic behind it, they can use it to close their positions and wait for the mean reversion to get invested again or search for a better, underpriced alternatives.

data.title: Ford Motor: Inventory Doesn't Lie
data.description:
F stock: previous thesis and Q4 recap

I last analyzed the Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) stock back in Oct 2024. More specifically, in an article entitled "The Ford Motor Absurdity: Yield Exceeds P/E", I argued for a buy thesis on the stock based on the absurd valuation the stock was trading at that time. Quote:

Market's moods occasionally swing to absurd degrees. In Ford's case, its current dividend yield (7%) now exceeds its FWD P/E of 6x. There are valid concerns over its warranty costs and quality issues. However, the market's projection of near-zero earnings growth in the next few years is too negative given Ford's strong ROCE and reinvestment rate.

Since then, there have been a few important changes surrounding F stock. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top catalyst on my list: its recent 2024 Q4 earnings report (ER), especially the inventory level reported during Q4. You will see why I believe the market has overacted to the headwinds the company reported in Q4, and thus reiterated my buy rating.

For readers unfamiliar with its Q4 performance, the next chart provides a summary. Overall, F demonstrated a strong performance in the latest quarter, exceeding expectations on both lines. It reported a normalized EPS of $0.39 (beating estimates by $0.07) and a GAAP EPS of $0.45, also surpassing estimates by $0.12. Revenue also impressed, reaching $44.94 billion and outperforming estimates by $2.11 billion. However, the ER was followed by downward price corrections and there are good reasons here. A few concerns linger underneath the updating numbers, including its conservative earnings outlook and also the pricing pressures on its EV products, especially the pressure from the Chinese OEMs.

My experience with highly cyclical automobile stocks like F is that many investors focus too much on the profits (especially accounting earnings) but overlook other more telling/leading indicators such as inventory. This is why we will take a closer look at its latest inventory data next.

F stock: inventory in focus

I learned the importance of analyzing inventory data following these insights I learned from Peter Lynch:

To start, unlike many other financial data that are more open to interpretation, inventory is one of the less ambiguous financial data. Lynch also explained why inventory levels can be a telltale sign of business cycles. Inventory buildup is a warning sign, which indicates the company (or sector) might be overproducing while the demand is already softening. Conversely, depleting inventory could be an early sign of a recovery.

Against this background, the chart below shows Ford's inventory levels observed in recent quarters. As seen, its inventory rose to a peak level of $18,632 million in the past 2~3 years in the March quarter of 2024. Its inventory fluctuated a bit since then, but the overall trend is downward. To wit, as of the most recent quarter, its inventory hovers around $14,951 million, the lowest level since March 2023.

To look at the above trend in a different way, the next chart depicts F's inventory in terms of Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO). As seen, the DIO has also fluctuated in the past few years, but the overall trend again is a declining trend. Over the past 5 years, the peak DIO was 50.28 days, observed in mid-2021. Currently, the DIO stands at 34.04 days, not only noticeably below the average DIO of 37.28 days but also among the lowest levels in at least 5 years.

The above inventory trends provide a clear indicator of the strong demand for F's vehicles, in my view. And indeed, several of its product lineups remain very popular. Leading the demand is its truck division in my model. Within this popular lineup, its F-Series (which includes the F-150 and Super Duty lines) have been reported double-digit YOY delivery advancements lately. This series has also been maintaining its crown as America's best-selling truck (for the 48th consecutive year and counting). Its hybrid automobiles are also enjoying high demand and have been reporting rapid sales growth lately.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few negatives/downside risks are worth mentioning. On the positive side, F is also trading at very attractive FEW P/E ratios. To wit, the chart below shows the consensus EPS estimates and also the implied P/E ratios for F stock in the next few years. As seen, analysts project Ford's EPS to be $1.40 for the incoming FY 2025, representing a large 24.08% decline YOY due to the headwinds mentioned above. Even with this decline, the FWD P/E is only 6.69x.

The EPS is then expected to recover and grow to $1.69 in 2026 and $1.77 in 2027, with growth rates of 21.19% and 4.27%, respectively. With these projections, the implied P/E ratios will decrease from 6.69 in 2025 to 5.52 in 2026 and only 5.29 in 2027.

On the negative side, as aforementioned, the competition on the EV front (especially from China) is a main risk. F's management is looking to address the competitive disruption from imported EVs (again mainly from China). I also feel there are some misalignments among regulations, infrastructure, and consumer demand for its EVs. The company also has some work to do to improve its competitive position in Europe, in my view. The company has plans to modify its cost structure to ensure the long-term growth and sustainability of its operations in the eurozone. In particular, the moves are aimed at turning around its passenger vehicle business, which has sustained significant losses in recent years. This plan also includes cutting the European workforce by 4,000 positions. The effectiveness of the measures is yet to be seen.

All told, my final verdict is that the upside far outweighs the downside under current conditions, leading to a reiteration of my buy rating on the stock despite the headwinds reported in its Q4 earnings. To recap, there are certainly some headwinds that could cause EPS decline in the near term. However, for highly cyclical stocks, I suggest potential investors look past accounting EPS and focus more on other indicators such as inventory. In F's case, its inventory data suggests strong demand for its products, and I expect such demand to persist given the popularity of its vehicle lineups.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Good Results But Even Warren Buffett's Not Buying (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
Buffett Replays The Greatest Hits

Warren Buffett's annual letter to Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) shareholders was once a must-read for its insightful commentary and detailed information on company operations for those lacking the time or financial expertise to read the full annual report on Form 10-K. I've been more critical of the letter in recent years, such as 2022 when Buffett greatly pared down information about the stock portfolio and performance in each of the company's segments. While the quality of the letter improved a bit in 2023, the 2024 letter again had a lot of rehashed material and topics that count as nice, but not necessarily helpful in evaluating one's investments. To name a few, we have:

  • Praise for an individual subsidiary manager and the story of how Berkshire came to acquire that business
  • A reminder the Berkshire pays the most federal taxes of any US corporation
  • America has been an impressive growth engine throughout its existence.
  • The annual meeting will be fun this year (though the Q&A will be shorter and not preceded by a movie.)

Once again, we will really need to dig into the 10-K to properly evaluate Berkshire. As I've been saying for years, the performance of Berkshire's operating divisions is now far more important than the management of the stock portfolio. In the letter, Buffett even redefines the meaning of "equities" to include fully owned businesses and downplay Berkshire's still-growing cash balance.

Despite what some commentators currently view as an extraordinary cash position at Berkshire, the great majority of your money remains in equities. That preference won’t change. While our ownership in marketable equities moved downward last year from $354 billion to $272 billion, the value of our non-quoted controlled equities increased somewhat and remains far greater than the value of the marketable portfolio.

Nevertheless, there are a few insights in the 2024 letter worth calling out before we get into the segment performance review. First is the clearer statement that Buffett's remaining tenure as CEO is coming to a close.

At 94, it won’t be long before Greg Abel replaces me as CEO and will be writing the annual letters. Greg shares the Berkshire creed that a “report” is what a Berkshire CEO annually owes to owners.

Maybe that is an obvious statement made with an eye on the actuarial tables, but in the past, Buffett has at least hedged about sticking around a little longer. We don't know if a handover timetable exists internally within Berkshire, but the odds are increasing that a transition could be announced soon. In all the speculation about the growing cash pile, "clearing the decks" for Abel has been offered as one possibility.

Second, the one insight from the letter into Berkshire's stock portfolio strategy is the revelation that the company is increasing its stake in Japanese trading companies beyond a previously agreed 10% limit. As these companies with low growth but strong free cash flow trade back into single-digit P/E territory, Buffett is increasing Berkshire's position.

Berkshire seems to be sticking to its value investing discipline, not paying up for growth or relaxing its standards just because the rest of the market (at least in the US) seemingly has.

Finally, the letter gives us two pages on property and casualty insurance underwriting, a meaty topic that used to be more characteristic of the whole letter. The insurance segment is clearly the reason why Berkshire did "better than expected" by Buffett in 2024, including a "spectacular" improvement at Geico. So, let's start there as we dig into the segment commentary in the 10-K.

Insurance

"Spectacular" is barely sufficient to describe the insurance business in 2024, as it earned $9 billion in underwriting income, compared to $5.4 billion in 2023. More impressively, the company delivered this performance despite higher significant catastrophic losses in 2024 ($1.2 billion from Hurricanes Helene and Milton) than in 2023 ($0.7 billion from storms in New Zealand and Italy). Berkshire also had lower gains in 2024 from revising down estimates of future losses that were made in prior years. Finally, within reinsurance, National Indemnity Co. (NICO) took a $0.5 billion charge related to a bankruptcy at a non-insurance affiliate.

Despite these negative one-offs, Berkshire still managed that impressive improvement in underwriting income. Geico is the key driver of this performance, where underwriting gains more than doubled to $7.8 billion from $3.6 billion (pre-tax). Inflation is not a bad thing for insurance companies, as it gives them the ability to raise rates. That was the main reason premiums earned grew at Geico, although the insurer also got back to positive growth in policy count in the second half of 2024 after a couple years of pullback. Geico also had lower claims frequency in 2024 as cars got safer.

The rest of Berkshire's primary insurance business had a decline in underwriting income, to $0.9 billion from $1.4 billion, but this illustrates Berkshire's underwriting discipline. The company exited admitted homeowners' insurance (where rates are regulated by state agencies) in response to insufficient rates and higher than expected losses at the GUARD division.

The reinsurance business has lumpy results, but grew by $0.8 billion in 2024 as unusually high asbestos and environmental claims estimates in 2023 did not recur.

Investment income improved from $9.6 billion in 2023 to $13.7 billion in 2024 thanks to the higher cash balance and interest rates.

Looking forward, 2025 already has a headwind of $1.3 billion (pre-tax) from the southern California wildfires. Still, I expect rate increases and policy count growth to continue driving improvements this year. Higher for longer short-term interest rates and the size of the cash balance will also help maintain strong investment income. The Insurance segment could have another record year if the upcoming hurricane season is not severe.

BNSF

As Buffett said in the letter, "Berkshire’s railroad and utility operations, our two largest businesses outside of insurance, improved their aggregate earnings. Both, however, have much left to accomplish."

BNSF had impressive volume growth with 6.5% more car loadings in 2024, especially in consumer and agricultural products and growing intermodal shipments at west coast ports. Coal was the only weak area. Revenues were flattish due to lower fuel surcharges, but on the cost side, lower fuel costs were partially offset by the cost of a labor union settlement in 4Q. This will improve efficiency in future years however, as it will allow the company to reallocate workers between different job classifications where needed to fill openings. The railroad also had a one-time charge for a legal settlement regarding volumes of oil tankers shipped across Native land in Washington state. Despite these negative one-offs, BNSF improved operating ratio (operating expenses / revenues) to 68.0% in 2024 from 68.4% in 2023.

In 2025, absence of these one-offs should lead to further improvements in the operating ratio. Tariffs are an item to watch as they could impact volumes of intermodal shipments through the ports. This could lead to increased domestic shipping, however.

BHE

Berkshire acquired the final minority interest in Berkshire Hathaway Energy in 2024 and now owns 100% of the segment. BHE will also be redeeming preferred stock this year, further increasing income attributable to Berkshire shareholders.

The big improvement in 2024 results came from a reduction in accrued legal settlements for wildfires in Oregon and Northern California in 2020. These costs dropped to $0.3 billion in 2024 from $1.7 billion in 2023. (Charges are before tax and net of expected insurance recoveries.) Operationally, the company benefitted from customer growth at US utilities, a full year of owning the Cove Point LNG terminal in Maryland, and lower tax accruals at the UK utilities.

In future years, settlement of the wildfire cases will remove a headwind, and growing electrical demand from data centers and reshoring of US manufacturing should benefit BHE as much as the publicly traded utilities.

Pilot

Pilot Travel Centers are now reported in the Service and Retailing division, but I am breaking them out separately here. The business earned $0.6 billion in 2024, in line with my expectations. Pilot lowered its interest expense by borrowing internally from the parent company rather than issuing its own debt on the open market.

Fuel margins will remain volatile in the future, but Pilot also has a small water disposal business in the oil fields sector. Pilot is also partnering with General Motors (GM) to add EV charging stations to its travel centers.

Manufacturing Service And Retail

Berkshire's Manufacturing group, taken as a whole, earned as much in 2024 as BNSF and BHE put together, around $9.5 billion. This group really deserves more attention. If it were a standalone company, I believe it would be worth around $171 billion in market cap. Sales on average increased 2.4% in 2024. The star of the group, however, was Precision Castparts (PCC), which grew sales 12% in 2024, not bad for the business that was the subject of a $10 billion impairment charge in 2020. Margins improved in almost every area of Manufacturing due to efficiency and lower raw material costs in many businesses. The exception was Clayton Homes, which had strong revenue growth but higher interest expense and manufacturing cost. Since Clayton Homes borrows mainly from the parent company, their interest expense shows up on the "Other" income line of the summary earnings table.

Service revenues were flattish in 2024, helped by NetJets and Flight Safety, and IPS, an architecture, engineering, and construction firm acquired from Alleghany. Retailing sales were down, showing the effects of a cautious consumer. Margins were also lower across the group due to higher SG&A costs except for food distributor McLane.

Capital Management

Berkshire's cash balance and sales from the stock portfolio have been discussed ad nauseam elsewhere this year, so I'll just highlight some key points. The cash flow statement showed $143 billion of stock sales in 2024 against only $9 billion in purchases. While Apple (AAPL) was the big story in the first part of the year, 4Q selling was really concentrated on Financials. I estimate about $7.7 billion of the $10.1 billion sold in 4Q was in Bank of America (BAC) and Citigroup (C). Capital One (COF) and NU Holdings (NU) accounted for about another $1 billion. Berkshire ended the year with $318 billion of cash in the Insurance & Other businesses, net of payables for T-Bills purchased but not yet settled at the end of the quarter. That is an increase of $12.5 billion since the end of Q3.

Berkshire reduced debt by $3.5 billion during the year despite adding about $3.7 billion in Yen denominated notes. These are used to finance the purchase of the Japanese trading company stocks and effectively hedge the currency risk. If the dollar strengthens as it did in 2024, the mark to market declines in the value of this debt shows up in the "Other" income line in the summary operating earnings.

At first glance, free cash flow looks bad at only $11.6 billion, compared to $30.5 billion in 2023, but Berkshire paid about $20 billion in cash taxes on capital gains of $101 billion from the stock sales during the year.

Berkshire did zero buybacks in 4Q and also bought back no B shares during the year. The only buybacks were $2.9 billion worth of A shares. The company had an unusual issuance of 2.3 million B shares (around $1 billion worth) as part of the acquisition of the minority stake in BHE.

Berkshire Hathaway Stock Valuation

My regular sum-of-the-parts valuation model is presented below.

Compared to 3Q, the non-insurance businesses are valued about $12 billion lower, mostly due to lower P/E's of comparable public companies. This is offset by a $13 billion gain in investment holdings, driven entirely by the higher cash balance. The big gain, around $35 billion, is in the Insurance segment, influenced mostly by the higher underwriting earnings.

My valuation estimate works out to $487.32 per B share, which is just 1.8% above the closing market price before the earnings release. The market price is now 1.59 times book value, within a very steady range of 1.53 to 1.61 it has traded at over the last four quarters.

Conclusion

Warren Buffett's annual letter to Berkshire Hathaway shareholders rehashed a lot of previously expressed ideas and provided little insight into the company. The 10-K was much more useful to investors, giving us needed details on each business. The Insurance segment had an outstanding year, with strong improvements at Geico. BNSF and BHE both had some one-off legal issues. Once these are resolved, the improvements in the underlying operations should become clearer. The Manufacturing group should not be overlooked, with earnings power as big as BNSF and BHE put together. Manufacturing continues to show steady growth and margin improvements from operating efficiency and raw material costs.

There's not much more to say about the stock portfolio and cash balance that has not already been said. Concerns over high valuations and a desire to clear the deck for an incoming Greg Abel are both plausible explanations.

The sum of the parts valuation shows that Berkshire is trading nearly at fair value. Adding in uncertainty over the macro economy and the timing of Buffett's succession, I consider Berkshire shares a Hold at this time. I remain confident that the stock should be able to outperform the S&P 500 in a bear market. However, if you think one is coming, why not just do what Buffett has been doing and raise your cash allocation instead of buying more Berkshire shares?

data.title: Nvidia's Stock May Drop After Earnings - Even If It Beats
data.description:
NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) (TSX:NVDA:CA) is expected to report results on February 26. In the fiscal fourth quarter, the company's revenue is estimated to have grown by 72.1% to $38.0 billion, with gross margins of 73.5%. This is expected to increase earnings by 63.5%, or $0.84 per share. However, an inline quarter would be a big disappointment, especially since investors know this company has reported better-than-expected results seven quarters in a row since May 2023.

In some ways, what Nvidia reports may not matter. Even if the company delivers a strong quarter, the options positioning could still lead to post-earnings selling pressure as market makers unwind hedges, forcing downward price action. We have seen this in the past, such as in the August and November quarters.

It's Never Enough For The Market

It isn't just whether they beat; it also depends on how much the company raises its guidance. Historically, it has increased its guidance by about $4 to $5 billion above the prior quarter's guidance, which has now happened six quarters in a row.

You get this predictable pattern of beating its guidance by approximately $2 billion seven quarters in a row and then raising its outlook by $2 billion above the current quarter's revenue.

For example, in the quarter that ended October 29, 2023, the company was guiding revenue to $16 billion and reported $18.12 billion, a roughly $2 billion beat. The company then issued guidance for the January 28, 2024 quarter, to $20 billion at the mid-point, about $4 billion above the October quarter's guidance and a raise of $2 billion above the October revenue. This has been the pattern since July 2023.

Based on this pattern, it would be no surprise to the market if the company reported around $40 billion in revenue during the current quarter and guided the market to around $42.5 billion at the midpoint of the guidance range for the coming quarter, which would be in line with the July and October 2024 quarters. Anything less could be considered a huge disappointment.

The question, of course, is when the pattern will break and whether or not this will be the quarter that happens. The sell-side analyst estimates have not picked up on this pattern and continue underestimating current quarter revenue and the following quarter's guidance. However, that gap has closed, with analysts estimating revenue for the coming April quarter at $41.751 billion.

To surprise the market, revenue will likely need to exceed $40 billion, and guidance will need to exceed $43 billion to override the impact of IV crush and dealer hedges being unwound that will occur after the news is released. Whether or not that is possible is unknown.

The Post-Earnings Volatility Crush

That IV crush is the biggest problem for Nvidia's stock. An option with an expiration date of February 28 at the $135 strike price currently has implied volatility of around 88%. That implied volatility is likely to continue to rise right up until the market closes on February 26. It could even be well over 100% by the time the market closes on February 26.

The options market suggests that Nvidia stock can move around 9% from that $135 strike pricing using a long straddle strategy. This means that the stock could be in a trading range of $122 to $148 following the results. The problem is that the IV will fall dramatically once the results come out, and that will cause the puts and the calls to lose value as the IV crashes. Depending on where the stock is positioned and how changes in open interest develop heading into results, the direction of the stock will be very fluid.

At Friday's close, at least most of the net delta of the positioning in Nvidia was positive. This means that the stock's biggest risk following the results is trading lower, as the calls lose value quickly and the overall delta value in the options declines.

The gamma profile is also weighted towards the calls and shows significant resistance levels at $140 and again at $150. This means it will be difficult for the stock to see a meaningful move to higher prices following the results. It will take a significant beat and raise results above and beyond what the market is used to for the stock to move beyond $150.

If Nvidia reports its usual beat and raise pattern, it will not be enough to lift the stock because even a $2 billion beat and $2 billion raise will be what the market has grown to expect based on the history of the last seven quarters. The positioning of the options will force market makers to start unwinding hedges due to the buildup in positive delta and gamma exposure, resulting in the selling pressure pushing the shares lower.

Technical Support and Resistance Levels

The technical chart suggests that a break of support around $130 could lead to a sharp decline in the shares, potentially heading back to $115 in the days following results. Meanwhile, momentum has turned negative as measured by the relative strength index, and the bar for Nvidia to rise following results is very high, with lots of resistance in the way.

For the stock to move substantially higher, it will likely take a revenue beat of more than $2 billion and a guidance raise of more than $2 billion. Even then, it still faces challenging resistance at $150.

The results are likely to have a significant impact not just on Nvidia, but also on the entire market.

data.title: Sega Sammy: Infinite Wealth, A Tough Basis Against New Yakuza Spinoff
data.description:
Sega Sammy (OTCPK:SGAMF) had a decent quarter. The Sonic the Hedgehog movie performed well, receiving a good rating from critics and actually delivering a great kids' movie that Sonic fans could also enjoy, carried by Jim Carrey. This supported the animation results within the entertainment segment. Sonic X Shadow Generations also performed well, as mentioned in our last coverage, hitting more than a million units very quickly and supporting together with ReFantazio probably more than 10% of the overall cumulative Q3 revenues. Timing effects have hit the pachinko segment's forecasts, where overall Sega Sammy, also on account of disposals, is revising down the guidance. Football Manager 2025 also got cancelled, which is quite bad news, but at least a new Yakuza game just hit the shelves and is likely to perform very well, with Yakuza being a darling franchise for Sega Sammy at this point. The issue is comps have gotten tougher. We understand the guidance downgrade and think that growth will be tough to post. It's not the cheapest pick in the space, but it's definitely getting its act together and has a really monetisable model with the Yakuza games and with the Altus games too, although nothing was released from the latter studio this latest period.

Latest Earnings

Sonic the Hedgehog 3 did really well at the box office, 4xing its budget at the worldwide box office, which clears it of the 2-2.5x rule of thumb for multiple profitability, given that budget doesn't include marketing etc. It was released late December before the close of books. There is a lot of outstanding contribution here for the coming quarter, since its opening was around $100 million worldwide within December, and made around $480 million globally in total. While the topline revision isn't that big, the latest forecast revisions reflect that the movie was a major commercial success, with animation operating income expected to close to double YoY. They've also announced they're doing a 4th film, which is not a surprise. We are a bit worried about how it will go if Jim Carrey doesn't come back as Robotnik.

Consumer is a little tougher. While Sonic X Shadow Generations and ReFantazio did well, next quarter is only seeing one major release of a new Yakuza game set in Hawaii. This is comparable to last year's Gaiden game, "The Man Who Erased His Name", which was a really successful Yakuza spin off that generated sales not that far off the headliner of the year, Like a Dragon: Infinite Wealth, which was a major commercial and critical success and cemented the Yakuza series as a major money-maker for Sega Sammy. The comp is going to be Infinite Wealth this time, which is tougher to beat than the Gaiden game that was released in Q3 of last year. Sonic X Shadow and ReFantazio together managed to beat out last year's Q3 for consumer, where Endless Dungeon, a product of the now sold Amplitude Studios, performed badly, as well as a really poor Total War release and a lukewarm success of Sonic Superstars. The Yakuza Gaiden game was the only bright spot last year, Q3. Virtua Fighter is a port and probably just a prelude to a more aggressive monetisation of that slightly dormant property, as part of Sega's new strategic plan. The good news is that Hawaii is doing really well just a couple of days after release. As of this writing, there are 17k players playing it on Steam, and the estimate is around 70-150k unit sales, where Gaiden's year-on value, close to the lifetime value for Steam sales, is around 500k. In terms of number of players at release, it is looking very similar to the Gaiden game, but not currently holding a candle to Infinite Wealth which was a main-line release, and not a spin-off game. As said, it will be tough to generate growth against that comp with the pipeline currently thin, hence the revision. Also, they have been continuing a restructuring this year and doing some disposals, also leading to less catalogue revenue to be recognised in the consumer segment.

Another significant cause of the revenue downgrade is also the cancellation of Football Manager 2025. They weren't ready in time to change the formula as promised with the next entry, and we are too far into the season for them to in good conscience release a game whose appeal is around a timely and updated database. A loss of an annual instalment cash cow for this year is really unfortunate. These releases are a lay-up to contribute around 25% of the revenues that come in from major blockbuster titles like Infinite Wealth.

In terms of operating profit, major cost-cutting and lack of major write-downs associated with the HYENAS calculation and other restructuring-related expenses also helped the operating income comps in the forecasts. While growth isn't expected, the downsizing of the business and scrutinising the studio portfolio at least means a better profit outlook.

For pachinko and pachislot, they really upfronted revenues into the previous year when a lot of new game machines were released, and parlours started to re-ignite investment along with the mandated machine changes that are periodic in the regulated gambling industry in Japan. Particularly, the newer pachislot machines were really a big contributor last year as the category shot up in adoption. They also postponed some launches meant for Q4, which is why the forecasts had to change. There is also the general demographic and secular decline associated with pachinko. Pachislot mitigates it as it is more appealing to a newer generation, but the industry is in decline, particularly as Japan possibly moves towards legalising more typical gambling, currently mostly illegal.

Bottom Line

In consumer, the pipeline is a little thin. Sega has options, but it will take time to exploit properties like Crazy Taxi, Shinobi and Virtua Fighter. Other anticipated releases haven't been announced yet, but things can come to market quite quickly, particularly from Altus, whose games are not as costly to develop. Nonetheless, the forecasts show no growth expectation, which is consistent with our tough comp view.

Longer-term, Sega Sammy is trying to build out an answer to the pachinko decline. They are entering the slot machine market with the GAN Ltd. acquisition and acquisition of Stakelogic to improve their B2B offering and to make a go at the large and still legalising US gambling market. They have basically just started producing slot machine with an intention on the US market, and through the Sammy business they want to counteract the demographic discount that the stock gets. Strategically, we see it as a sound move.

In terms of valuation, Sega Sammy is around a 9x EV/EBITDA multiple with Electronic Arts (EA) at over 13x, a decent comp because of its annual franchises as well as other beloved (although lately more miss than hit) franchises like Dragon Age. We think Sega is more quality and has more easily addressed areas for improvement, including their business development in the gambling business already underway. We think there is a relative case, though with a lot of the good news already passed this year, such as the Sonic movie release and other easy hits like Sonic X Shadow Generations, where Shadow as a character is always a major draw, the direction is more troubled now and the pipeline less exciting. In other words, no major catalysts at the moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Birkenstock: A Great Dip To Buy As Margins March Upward
data.description:
In my view, this earnings season in particular is a great time to deploy active investing. A number of attractive growth stocks are on sale following weak post-earnings reactions, opening up an opportunity to increase our exposure to “growth at a reasonable price” stocks while prices offer a bit more valuation cushion.

Birkenstock Holding (NYSE:BIRK), in my view, is one of these names. The German shoemaker has seen its share price decline ~10% since the start of the year, with losses picking up after the company's latest fiscal Q1 earnings print - which showed a beat to in-quarter estimates, but investors reacted harshly to the company's expectations for a growth deceleration in FY25 - which, as usual, likely contains some level of conservatism. In my view, this dip is a great juncture to buy the stock.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Birkenstock in January, when the stock was still trading near $57. Since then, amid the ~10% decline in the company's share price on top of still-strong growth and an improving margin profile, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Birkenstock - especially as the company saw improved growth in its B2B segment, which had been one of my earlier key concerns. This is a company that has managed to consistently hold on to a ~20% growth CAGR for years, despite being a brand with over one hundred years of history - showcasing how much greenfield market space that Birkenstock still has left to cover.

To me, these are the key drivers of the bus case for Birkenstock:

  • Birkenstock boasts incredible ~60% gross margins. For a consumer products maker, Birkenstock's low-60s gross margin profile is sky-high, giving the company incredible room for operating leverage as it scales. It's equally impressive that Birkenstock manages these high gross margins without being a luxury brand (its standard clogs range from $100-$160; though certainly not cheap, a Birkenstock product is not at all out of reach, but rather an accessible luxury).
  • Incredible Asia growth. Asia is Birkenstock's biggest growth market, especially in China - where the company has managed ~50% growth. The continued success of Birkenstock's expansion in China signals a much wider market opportunity that will help to sustain growth as more mature markets in Europe slow down.
  • Continued innovation. Though Birkenstock's brand and especially its signature clogs are timeless, the company has also found success in releasing new styles, particularly closed-toed shoes and boots, that have helped to drive continued revenue growth.

Stay long here and hold out for a rebound.

Q1 recap

Let's now go through Birkenstock's latest fiscal Q1 (December quarter) results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Birkenstock's revenue grew 19% y/y to €367.1 million, beating Street expectations of €356.2 million (+18% y/y) by a one-point margin, while also decelerating from 22% y/y revenue growth in Q4. We note that what investors and the Street reacted to more harshly was the fact that Birkenstock merely affirmed, rather than raised, its full-year outlook calling for 15-17% y/y constant-currency growth, implying further deceleration down the road this fiscal year. And while I acknowledge that the quarter wasn't perfect, and I'd certainly have appreciated a guidance raise, I think there are positive mitigating offsets (least of which is Birkenstock's better valuation proposition for investors post-earnings).

The chart below showcases Birkenstock's sales by channel and region:

The good news here: Asia Pacific continued at a blazing growth pace, up 47% y/y to €47 million in revenue, or barely 15% of Birkenstock's total - indicating plenty of room for further expansion.

The other positive point I liked about this quarter: B2B revenue re-accelerated to 30% y/y growth, from 26% y/y growth in Q4. I had initially been worried about a softer retail environment which had called many resellers and retailers to pull back on re-orders, which in the end has the impact of making brands less visible to consumers during the critical holiday period. Management notes that sell-through was strong and that its retail partners committed to strong reorders, per CEO Oliver Reacher's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Revenue growth was supported by double-digit volume growth and mid-single digit growth in average selling price.

Growth in our B2B business benefited from very strong sell-through and reorders from our key wholesale partners throughout the holiday season. And our DTC business also strengthened during the critical holiday shopping period as strong gifting demand for styles such as the Boston clog led to record traffic to our websites.

We are starting fiscal 2025 much in the same way we exited fiscal 2024 with strong and growing demand from our core markets and products by leaning into the white space growth opportunities we have identified, closed-toe shoes, the APAC region and owned retail. During the quarter, revenue from closed-toe silhouettes grew at over twice the rate of the overall group and increased share of business by 600 basis points. Accounting for well over half of the revenue in the quarter, our peak winter quarter."

The flip side of this, however, is that Birkenstock's direct channel business slowed to 10% y/y growth, from 18% growth in Q4 - which I view as the purest read on the brand's growth, as it ignores the impact of channel inventory loading. There is a possibility here that reseller orders may have been a bit too strong, and that the ~30% growth represents resellers ordering and increasing inventory ahead of sell-through: which may unwind and drive revenue deceleration in future quarters. We'll have to watch how the B2B business trends (especially relative to DTC) as well as management's commentary on sell-through closely in the quarters ahead.

We note that on the profitability front, Birkenstock's adjusted EBITDA margins rose 130bps y/y to 28.2%.

Note that this is in spite of a 70bps y/y headwind in gross margins (which reduced to a still-high 60.3%, driven primarily by the greater revenue mix into the B2B channel and out of full-priced DTC sales). We additionally like the fact that Birkenstock's on-hand inventory is also reducing as a percentage of revenue (to 37%, down 3 points y/y) - which to me indicates that the company is moving through stock more efficiently, and will be less exposed to the risk of aging styles that have to be written off.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $52, Birkenstock trades at a market cap of $9.73 billion. After we net off the €298.6 million of cash and €1.20 billion of debt on Birkenstock's latest balance sheet ($949 million of net debt at today's exchange rate of $1.05 to the euro), the company's resulting enterprise value is $10.68 billion.

The company's reiterated guidance of 15-17% y/y revenue growth implies €2.09 billion in revenue ($2.19 billion), and the midpoint of its adjusted EBITDA margin range of 30.8-31.3% gets us €650 million in adjusted EBITDA (+17% y/y, or $683 million in U.S. dollar terms).

As such, Birkenstock trades at 15.6x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. To me, for a company that is still growing adjusted EBITDA at an expected ~20% y/y pace, with meaningful growth drivers from continued DTC penetration and expansion into high-growth regions like Asia, and a rich ~60% gross margin profile, I think Birkenstock trades at a reasonable price.

Stay long here and use the post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Wabtec: Continued Margin Execution
data.description:
In the final days of 2024 I believed that it was time to blow off some steam in the case of Wabtec (NYSE:WAB). The company had seen a strong operating performance, evident in terms of revenue growth, margin gains, supplemented by M&A activities.

While this was to be applauded, multiples had risen to 25 times earnings, making me more cautious on the shares. Shares have stagnated a bit, after a resilient fourth quarter, but this was accompanied by a sound (earnings) outlook for 2025 and an interesting deal.

This makes me more fond of the business, looking for a further dip from here, as earnings growth is rapidly reducing high valuations to more acceptable multiples here.

Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies

Wabtec has been rallying to fresh highs in 2024 after the diversified railroad equipment provider has seen improved operating performance last year, with performance finally picking up real steam, years after the company made a transformative acquisition of GE's Transportation assets pre-pandemic.

Over time, the company has grown to become a ten billion leader in freight and transit technologies, with sales generated across the world by no less than 30,000 workers. Some 70% of these sales are generated in the freight segment, which on their term are comprised out of equipment, components and digital intelligence. This is complemented by a transit segment, responsible for the remainder of sales.

Some 60% of revenues are generated in the Americas, most of which in the US, complemented by activities in the EMEA region and the wider Asia-Pacific region. Most customers are railroad and transit business, accompanied by smaller clientele in industrial and mining applications, with much of the sales being recurring and/or aftermarket services.

A focus in on innovation and technology was complemented by the need to refresh a global installed base, as rail could play a major role in decarbonizing transport.

Expectations Arise

For the year 2023, Wabtec posted an impressive 15% increase in annual sales to $9.7 billion, with GAAP operating margins equal to 13.1% of sales resulting in GAAP earnings of $4.53 per share, and adjusted earnings reported at $5.92 per share. Most of the adjustments between both earnings metrics stems from amortization charges, which I am happy to adjust for.

While sales were strong, the total backlog fell by 2% to $22.0 billion, but despite the falling backlog the company guided for 2024 sales to improve to $10.2 billion, with earnings seen close to $6.70 per share. This came amidst a modest net debt load of $2.7 billion, equal to 1.5 times EBITDA reported around $1.8 billion.

Shares rose from a low of $140 early in 2024 to a high of $210 around the time of the election, and shares ended the year near the $190 mark. This was supported by no less than three sequential increases in the full-year outlook, with earnings seen around $7.55 per share alongside the third quarter earnings report, which valued equity at 25 times earnings.

The company furthermore announced two tuck-in acquisitions in the Transit business, adding just over $100 million in annual sales. This came after the business operated with $3.1 billion in net debt, equal to 1.4 times EBITDA of $2.2 billion. Given the elevated earnings multiple and a modest 3% increase in the backlog to $22.2 billion so far in 2024, I was leaning cautious.

A Big Deal

Since the start of the year, shares hit a high of $210 in recent weeks, and now have sold off to $185 per share. The sell-off came despite what's quite eventful on the corporate front. In the final days of 2024, Wabtec announced a $69 million bolt-on deal to acquire Bloom Engineering, a provider of industrial heating applications.

Halfway January, Wabtec announced a big $1.78 billion deal to acquire Evident's Inspection Technologies, part of Olympus. The acquired activities are known for industry-leading brands and reference-based technologies, and will add to Wabtec´s Digital Intelligence business. This company employs some 1,300 workers which generate revenues from non-destructive testing, remote visual inspection and analytical instrument revenues.

After tax benefits, the purchase price drops by a hundred million to $1.68 billion, as the company will add $433 million in sales and $112 million in EBITDA. This values the activities at 3.9 times sales and 15 times EBITDA, as the latter multiple will drop to 12 times if we factor in an anticipated $25 million in annual cost synergies. This should result in modest accretion to 2025 earnings, although that this has not been quantified.

Later in the month, the company announced a $248 million locomotive order in east Guinea and by the middle of February, Wabtec announced its fourth quarter results. Fourth quarter sales rose by a modest 2% to $2.58 billion, being softer than a 7% increase reported in full year sales to $10.39 billion, as the annual backlog rose just a percent to $22.2 billion. The company reported full year GAAP earnings of $6.04 per share, and adjusted earnings to $7.56 per share, up from less than six dollar a year before.

With a share count of 172 million shares trading at $185, Wabtec´s equity is valued at $32 billion, or close to $35 billion if we factor in a $2.8 billion net debt load. This values the business at 3.4 times sales and 16 times EBITDA of $2.1 billion. In that light, the purchase of Evident looks to take place at quite attractive valuation multiples.

Pro forma net debt will jump to $4.5 billion, for a leverage ratio around 2 times, as this might drop following an anticipated improvement in EBITDA for 2025.

Another Solid Year In The Making - 2025

For the current year, Wabtec sees full year sales between $10.72 and $11.02 billion, with revenues up a modest 4%. More impressive is that adjusted earnings are seen around $8.55 per share, plus or minus twenty cents. Important to stress in this respect is that the purchase of Evident is not included in this guidance.

This guidance might look a bit soft, but the company announced a new 5-year guidance which calls for mid-single digit revenue growth as well as 350 basis points of anticipated margin expansion. Such margin gains on the current revenue base alone might add about $2 per share to earnings, absent of revenue growth, creating a real runway for earnings to grow over $10 per share in the coming years.

Based on the current earnings outlook, shares trade at 21-22 times forward earnings, quite a reduction from a mid-twenty times multiple in December. What has changed is that of a substantial deal, as announced in January, one which will jack up net leverage ratios to just over 2 times. Leverage has the potential to come down rapidly, despite a 25% increase in the quarterly dividend, now payable at $1.00 per share, for a very modest payout ratio in the teens.

After a softer fourth quarter, and much more modest growth in the backlog, I am still somewhat cautious here as forward earnings multiples are still somewhat demanding, although that potential for continued margin gains in the coming year provide clear roadmap for earnings to surpass $10 per share by 2026/2027.

If that were to happen, I am inclined to buy the dip here. While the current pullback is relatively modest, a great pullback, let's say to levels in the $160-$170s, looks enticing enough to get involved.

data.title: Pagaya: Is It Safe To Get In This Water
data.description:
Pagaya: Are the decks cleared for action or is still avoiding past torpedoes

Pagaya (NASDAQ:PGY) reported its operating results for Q4 and provided its outlook for 2025. At some level, the results were decent-depends on how one defines that word, of course. The outlook was quite strong as well. That matters at some level-but the real issue for the company and the stock is whether it has reached the end of its write-offs and impairment charges. Management makes clear that it belies it has-others are doubtless more skeptical.

Our book of older vintages, spanning 2023, 2022 and 2021, which have adversely impacted profitability over the past few quarters, are no longer expected to materially impact our performance. This lifts our level of visibility with the expected reduction in future impairments going forward

Other issues are how did the company come to a place where write-offs had to be made? How can investors be sure it won’t happen again? And is the company now being completely transparent about all of the risks and uncertainties in its business? Finally, what is the longer-term outlook for the company? Will its ultimate outcome be worth the risk and uncertainties?

I have owned Pagaya shares for some time and have written positive articles about the outlook for the company. Owning and recommending the shares has been anything but a pleasant experience. I hate to use the trite phrase-learning experience-but the journey has indeed been that for me. Based on what the company’s results were, its specific guidance, the willingness of management to accept responsibility for the results of past periods, and my understanding of the company’s technology, market opportunities and its many productive partnerships I think the lesson has been learned and the issues of the past are unlikely to be repeated. I believe that despite the share price spike on Thursday, February 13 and beyond, the shares are a buy-and a better buy now at some level than they were before the share price spike.

In writing this article, I am going to assume that readers have a bit of basic background on this story and this company. In the simplest terms, Pagaya is a sub-prime lending enabler that uses AI to underwrite loans. Specifically, the company has over 30 lending partners who originate loans, over 130 capital partners who buy ABS used to fund loans and a number of specific relationships with capital partners who either buy loans directly (pass-throughs) or who fund forward flow agreements. The company gets a fee for its underwriting technology. Depending on structure, it gets a fee for the creation and sale activity of ABS.

It makes loans in 3 categories these days-personal loans are 60% of the total, Auto loans are about 25% of the total, and what Pagaya calls POS loans are 15% of the total, although rising rapidly both absolutely and as a percentage of the total loan book. Auto loans and POS loans are likely to grow far more rapidly than personal loans. The company has 8 loan origination partners who are each responsible for more than $500 million in network volume.

Pagaya’s operational performance and its share price has been substantially impacted by impairment charges the company has taken over the past few quarters. I am not going to review the fair value adjustment metrics in detail. They can be seen on pg. 20 of the company’s disclosure of supplemental financial information. Simply put, that is a lot of impairment for a company of this size.

What happened? Much of the impairment charges taken last quarter related to loans that were underwritten in 2023-last quarter, 90% of the charge was from loans made in that period.

What happened that led to such a massive charge, essentially wiping out any margin the company had originally reported on those loans. There is a simple answer, but it will be unpalatable to some. In order for the company to continue to scale its business that year, it sold ABS tranches in which there was little or no buffer for loan losses. Capital markets were difficult to access that year as the Fed dramatically tightened credit. Capital market investors wanted to get paid for interest rate risk-and in order to make that happen, the yield that Pagaya had to promise was sometimes equal to its expected return from the loans it was underwriting. As those loans started to season, and were evaluated by auditors, their value was marked down.

Hindsight is 20/20 as the saying goes. Should Pagaya have made those loans with the guard rails in place in 2023? Or should it have pulled back and paused its growth strategy. Or could it have done a better job in finding capital partners who would have funded the company’s loan creation with better/more accurate guard rails? Of course, option 3 is the desirable answer-but it may not have been a realistic strategy at the time. The company’s leadership acknowledged it should have done a better in optimizing results despite the difficult environment, but this is a stock recommendation and not a history lesson or a management critique.

I have recommended and owned Pagaya shares since the summer of 2023. I did not know about buffers, and DQ assumptions and net interest margins at a granular for this company at that time. The company didn’t make that information available with the granularity needed to evaluate the risks properly. It didn’t discuss the scenarios it was tracking in its investor presentations. Transparency was lacking.

And I will say that I didn’t push hard enough in my discussions with the company on that issue-I was more focused on how the company could manage the funding of its risk retention requirement, which was 5% on the ABS that it was selling.

Hindsight in that regard shows that looking at credit quality ought to be first priority in evaluating the investment merits of a company making loans. Affirm (AFRM), for example, has been run looking at credit quality as a first priority. Nothing else is going to matter-and nothing else has mattered-if there is an issue in evaluating credit, and that was obviously exacerbated by the difficult credit conditions of 2023.

Why did Pagaya do what it did? Leaving aside innuendos and conspiracy theories-and there are plenty of those around, including the "analysis" published just recently by an activist short, Iceberg, the company needed to scale; it has a relative substantial level of operating costs, and it needed more data to train its models so that they would better predict loan outcomes.

On the conference call last Thursday morning, the company’s senior management, as I mentioned earlier, did a mea culpa with regard to what happened during 2023. They explicitly talked about disclosure and underwriting, capital costs and the need to be able to produce satisfactory results throughout a credit cycle. To be fair, DQ rates for 2023 vintage underwritings have improved compared to the past. And they have been stable or improving over the last several quarters, as can be seen on slides 16/17 of the company’s supplemental information slide deck. The company has been prudent in evaluating loans. It received $197 billion of loan apps in Q4 and wound up approving just a bit over 1% of those applications.

The Specifics of Pagaya’s most recent reported quarter:

KPIs for the quarter were as follows:

Network Volume of $2.6 billion, up by 9% and at the top of the guided range.

Revenues of $279 million, about 5% greater than prior expectations and growth of 28%

Revenue less production cost (equivalent to gross margins for a company such as this) at $117 million, up 55% year over year and 4.5% of network volume

Adjusted EBITDA of $54 million of $64 million-up 88% year-on-year. The company reported GAAP operating income of $32 million and non-GAAP adjusted income of $13 million.

Credit related impairments of $229 million in total. The company’s report makes this statement:

The company does not expect investments from 2021 to 2023 vintages to have a material impact on its performance going forward. Cumulative net losses (“CNLs”) for our 2023 personal loan vintages are 20 - 35% lower than peak levels in 2021, while CNLs for our 2023 auto vintages are 35 - 50% lower than those peak levels we saw in 2022.

The company has forecast that it will be GAAP profitable from Q2-2025 and beyond.

The specifics of Pagaya’s forecast are as follows:

The company is forecasting full-year network volume of $11 billion at the midpoint of its range. At the mid-point, network volume would be up by 13.5% year-on-year.

Revenues are forecast to be about $1.22 billion, again at the midpoint of the range. This would be growth of 18%.

The company is forecasting adjusted EBITDA of around $280 mil. for 2025. This compares to $210 mil. of adjusted EBITDA in 2024.

The company is forecasting GAAP net income of $25 million for 2025. GAAP net income last year was ($401 mil.) Within the GAAP net income forecast is an assumption shown on Slide 21 of the earnings supplement that is important to understand. It shows that the GAAP net income forecast includes a full-year credit impairment charge of $100-$150 million. Last year, the impairment charge was $354 million. The company expects a significantly smaller impairment charge than shown in its forecast but chose to use a metric that gives it significant room to over-attain.

These results show significantly less growth than Upstart (UPST), the most comparable competitor in the market for AI underwritten personal loans. It is my belief that Pagaya, after the nightmare period through which it has passed during which it incurred impairment charges of $354 million, is particularly sensitive to investor concerns about that kind of a result. The company is probably using exceptionally tight credit guard rails to avoid any reprise of that kind of result.

I will discuss the company’s growth paths below. It is worth noting that the company’s most mature product, personal loans, actually grew 24% last quarter. The company’s newest product category, POS, grew 176% sequentially, but that was only to $1 billion of annual run rate revenues. The company constrained auto lending-it must have been down significantly year-on-year, although not actually reported by the company. That said, auto lending, which was at a run rate of less than $1 billion for the quarter, grew sequentially by just under 40%. Auto lending, as shown in DQ rates, was obviously an Achilles' heel for this company in terms of loan losses and its models needed retooling and recalibration. It was the appropriate strategy to pause growth in loan origination until the net interest margins on auto loans reached a positive contribution margin.

Has Pagaya learned its lesson?

The world is not all black and white. People make mistakes of varying kinds and degrees. I certainly have and will make mistakes. I believe, based on the conference call commentary, and the shareholder letter, that this management team is very chastened and very aware of its past errors and what it has to do to ensure that they are not repeated. Here are some of the remediation strategies that the company is employing.

The first of these is evaluating credit. Had I wanted to write this article differently, I might have started with the company’s AI creds. The fact is that what makes Pagaya and some peers such as Affirm and Upstart a bit unique is the quality of their models. AI is an iterative process. The more data, the better the correlations. Overall, as the combination of AI and fully automated loan application processes have begun to gain significant traction in the personal loan space, there have been some teething issues. The teething issues were clearly exacerbated because of the spike in interest rates that culminated at the end of 2023. But it is safe to say that the models that the company had been using initially were not as accurate as they needed to be-and I think, based on the available evidence of DQ rates, that the models being used now to create loans are much more accurate and better reflect default risks.

There will always be risks and uncertainties in evaluating credit. AI does provide and is providing better forecasts than past paradigms-particularly credit scores. Pagaya has been tightening its credit standards for 18 months or more and the results are starting to show. The fact is that the company’s credit partners come to Pagaya because it has models that are more accurate than they themselves are likely to create-at least in a finite time span. The reason is quite simple: because Pagaya has so many large FI partners, it gets a plethora of credit applications and can look at a great number of events including defaults, and it can use its AI technology to best forecast credit outcomes.

At this point, it is probably worth providing a few comments about models, AI, and credit default risks. Some of this may be overly simplistic, but perhaps bears repetition when so much has been written about the models of Pagaya and Upstart and many other companies using AI as their principal tool to evaluate credit and forecast credit outcomes. I should make clear that these models and the AI used to create them are so-called “statistical models” and not generative AI.

The use of models, or what might also to be called multiple linear regressions, has been around for more than 60 years at this point. To be clear, this is not GenAI and these models are built without specific independent reasoning ability.

It is all about correlations. If an individual has an annual income of $1 million, a bank account with $200k and wants to obtain a $5k loan, there should be a high probability of repayment. But what if the individual has $2 million of debt, a mortgage of $5 million and if his job is in a high-risk industry, and he is 72 years old with chronic health issues and has a dependent sibling. The use of models based on historical correlations of similarly situated individuals and their past behavior can help predict if this borrower has a good or poor chance of repaying.

In the past, personal credit evaluations have been made using credit scores. This is a very crude tool. In the example above, the individual is likely to have a very high credit score, assuming his/her payments on his/her debt have been made on a timely basis and there have not been any recent credit applications. But the use of a raw credit score probably does not provide an appropriate answer in terms of making a loan and its expected net interest margin.

Credit models have hundreds of variables including economic conditions, age borrower location, marital status, dependent children, educational attainment and use of loan proceeds, past credit history, past income history and on and on. Data Scientists will run these correlations again and again and again, seeing which event or combination of events adds to the accuracy of a prediction. The process is a bit mechanical, it is iterative, and it is tedious-or at least it was so when I used to try to build models.

Models are in use and have been for many different things and have been so for decades at this point-really since the broad availability of computing. Weather forecasts are built on models. Economic forecasts are built on models-that happens to be my area of knowledge. The dynamic pricing models that set air fare are based on models. New products are often designed through the use of models, testing their prices and features. Models these days are often used in supply chain applications to optimize inventory and economic order quantities. For some years now, sports teams have used models to determine player value and which player bests fits into the potential results of a particular team. A film, Moneyball, was shot a dozen years ago describing some primitive elements of the process.

These days, few professional athletic teams could function without significant analytic inputs, although the field is so complex that the accuracy of predictions leaves much to be desired. Players get injured, and their performance is often a function not of their own specific attainments but of the performance of other players as part of a holistic experience.

One of the reasons for the fantastic success of AppLovin (APP) is that its Axon 2.0 has been so successful in forecasting the efficacy of in-game ads. Simply put, the AppLovin Axon 2.0 model has developed a high degree of correlation between the games users play and their interest in specific ad content for other games, and that will soon extend to products offered by on-line merchants. This has allowed ad buyers to achieve high levels of ROI. Because of high ROI, AppLovin users run more ads on the network, and that in turn provides the models with more data points and leads to more accurate correlations that further improve the ROI of using the model.

There is something similar going on when it comes to credit models-and particularly the credit models used by Pagaya to forecast the outcomes of the vast quantity of applications generated by its credit partners. Pagaya has different models for each credit partner. As it onboards each partner, and develops repayment information for each class of borrowers, its models become more accurate, and it is able to ramp volume from its new credit partners. The recent results and guidance are an outcome from that process.

There are no doubt many readers, who after the experience of the past several quarters in which Pagaya had to mark down the value of its loan portfolio substantially, are dubious about the results of loans originated in 2024 and future loans. In my view, the use of statistical AI models, will over time, with more loan applications and repayment events to consider from a wide array of different lending partners, almost inevitably see continued improvement in the predictive quality of these models. That said, as mentioned earlier, the company does present charts showing the performance of loans and their cumulative defaults as part of its supplemental financial presentation so that investors can see trends for themselves.

Many readers will be thinking at this point-if weather forecasts are an example of the use of statistical AI as a forecasting tool, they can see how models can be wildly inaccurate. I, personally, look at economic forecasts much the same way. Here I am in NYC going through the coldest winter since 2014, apparently, when before the start of this winter, it was forecast to be 2-3 F above normal.

Forecasting economic metrics is an equally fraught undertaking. I am sometimes bemused by articles on SA that, without the slightest pretense of being based on models, purport to forecast variables such as monthly inflation rates.

The current state of data science simply doesn’t allow for universally accurate weather forecasts or economic forecasts, or forecasting the performance of professional athletes. Predictive credit models will surely make mistakes, but the more data that operate on and the longer the timescale the more accurate they will become. And that is really the story for Pagaya, for Upstart for Affirm and other financial institutions who use statistical models in different ways to optimize results.

At this point, betting against Pagaya achieving satisfactory operational results is essentially equivalent to betting against the accuracy and the efficacy of their models. I believe that by now their models, along with those of Affirm, Upstart and others, are far more likely than not to be reasonably accurate, and their accuracy will almost certainly continue to improve over time.

They (credit evaluation models) are far more likely to be accurate than using rather crude tools such as credit scores-but again, not always. Properly used, and over a large enough sample of instances, they are likely to produce reasonable but not perfect results. The use of appropriate guard rails is key in order to avoid undesirable outcomes-and while most data scientists understand this all too well, that doesn’t mean that these guard rails have been or will be applied appropriately, especially given other corporate priorities.

I have doubtless oversimplified the process here, and in doing so, have perhaps unintentionally dissed data scientists and the skills and insights they bring to their demanding profession. But the point is that creating models is not some new, esoteric technology that might be seen as risky or with an uncertain outcome.

Pagaya’s business has been based on building models to predict credit outcomes. In that regard, it is little different from the models that are used by Affirm and Upstart and some other FIs to drive their businesses. In fact, because Pagaya’s business is based on getting loan applications from a multiplicity of large FIs, it probably has more data which can be used to train its models than is available for many other credit enablers at this point.

In asking the question as to whether Pagaya has learned its lesson-the key is what lesson should be learned. And the answer is straightforward. The absolute priority is to deploy better models that predict outcomes accurately. Doing so is going to provide operating results that are optimized for net interest margins, including charge offs and for the continued growth in loan originations, network volume, reported revenues and revenue less production costs. And that is exactly what Pagaya’s current strategy is designed to accomplish.

It has been a very expensive lesson to learn-and I say that as a shareholder as well as an SA contributor.

The learnings for all of us from our execution over the challenging 2021-2023 period, which was disappointing, are instructive, and we are committed to providing continued visibility to all our constituents going forward and do everything in our power to not have history repeat itself. Providing GAAP profitability guidance is a critical step towards that commitment.

And yes, I am reasonably confident that this is a lesson Pagaya’s leadership understands-in spades, really. The mea culpa here is as specific to any internal critique as anything I typically see. I own the shares now-basically because I think the strategy and the model make sense, the size of the opportunity is substantial and because the men who run the company seem to have learned their lesson

Pagaya’s Business Model: Potentially Capital Light and potentially very profitable

Pagaya’s strategy over the past several years has been to build a company that is capital efficient with high and sustainable margins. Many readers at this point are probably going to comment that its results have belied such a strategy, and I wouldn’t take the opposite side of that argument. But strategy and execution are two very different elements of the evolution of a business. My view is that Pagaya has the right strategy-but until just now, the strategy has been marked by poor execution coupled with a lack of transparency that has made progress hard to see.

There are a couple of parts of the strategy-one of which is somewhat unique, and the other which is only now starting to show some results. As compared to other fintech companies, Pagaya has multiple large fintech partners who provide it with loan applications. The largest disclosed fintech partner at this point is probably US Bank, which uses Pagaya technology to evaluate both personal loans and what are called POS loans-the buy now/pay later loans that are offered by US Bank through its Elavon subsidiary.

Some prominent loan origination partners include Ally Bank, SoFi, OneMain Financial, Best Egg, and Avant. It also has a partnership with Klarna, its initial foray into BN/PL lending. And it has partnerships with Visa and MasterCard for member banks of those companies who want to establish a BN/PL program.
